function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Segment Adjusted EBITDA is the filing’s primary non-GAAP measure. Relative to segment operating income, identify each add-back and explain why understanding construction of the metric matters before comparing segment economics.

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about SpaceX's S-1 filing, specifically about Segment Adjusted EBITDA as a non-GAAP measure. I need to:

1. Find the S-1 filing
2. Identify the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation
3. Identify each add-back relative to segment operating income
4. Explain why understanding the construction matters before comparing segment economics

Let me start by parsing the S-1 filing.

I need to see each add-back relative to segment operating income, including items like depreciation, amortization, stock-based compensation, restructuring, and any other adjustments. Also include the company's stated definition and rationale for this non-GAAP measure.

results.content:

Depreciation and amortization 2. Share-based compensation 3. Restructuring charges 4. Impairment **Rationale:** Management believes that certain financial measures not presented in accordance with GAAP provide management and investors with useful supplemental information. This provides a meaningful view of the company's financial condition and results of operations across periods by removing the impact of items that management believes do not directly reflect ongoing operating performance. Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA are key performance measures that management uses to assess financial performance, as well as for internal planning and forecasting purposes. The company considers them to be meaningful performance measures for investors to evaluate operating performance and compare financial results. **Year Ended December 31, 2024** | (in millions) | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total Reportable Segments | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Income (loss) from operations | $21 | $2,006 | $(1,561) | $466 | | Add: | | | | | | Depreciation and amortization | 637 | 1,508 | 1,679 | 3,824 | | Share-based compensation | 472 | 296 | 16 | 784 | | Restructuring charges | — | — | 213 | 213 | | Impairment | 24 | 39 | — | 63 | | **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$1,154** | **$3,849** | **$347** | **$5,350** |
**Year Ended December 31, 2023** | (in millions) | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total Reportable Segments | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Income (loss) from operations | $(1) | $469 | $(3,973) | $(3,505) | | Add: | | | | | | Depreciation and amortization | 571 | 884 | 1,180 | 2,635 | | Share-based compensation | 427 | 249 | 3 | 679 | | Restructuring charges | — | — | 237 | 237 | | Impairment | — | — | 3,775 | 3,775 | | **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$997** | **$1,602** | **$1,222** | **$3,821** |

steps.reasoning_content: I have the key information. Let me also retrieve more context about the rationale and any limitations disclosed by the company, as well as the segment definitions to better explain why understanding the construction matters.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, find any disclosure about the limitations of Segment Adjusted EBITDA, the company's discussion of why these adjustments are made, and any caveats about comparing segment economics. Also find the segment definitions for Space, Connectivity, and AI segments, and any discussion of how shared costs or common-control accounting affects segment results.

results.content:
Based on the provided text chunks, here is the relevant disclosure and discussion regarding your requests:

**1. Limitations of Segment Adjusted EBITDA**
The text explicitly states the following regarding limitations:
> "Segment Adjusted EBITDA differently than we do or may not calculate them at all, limiting their usefulness as comparative measures. Because of these limitations, Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA should not be considered in isolation or as a substitute for net income (loss), income (loss) from operations, or any other measure calculated in accordance with GAAP, and should be considered together with our GAAP financial measures and the reconciliations to the corresponding most directly comparable GAAP financial measures set forth"

**2. Why These Adjustments Are Made**
The company explains the purpose and rationale behind these non-GAAP measures as follows:
> "Adjusted EBITDA is defined as net income (loss) excluding (i) depreciation and amortization, (ii) share-based compensation, (iii) impairment, (iv) restructuring charges, (v) interest expense, (vi) interest income, (vii) other income (expense), net and (viii) provision for income taxes. Segment Adjusted EBITDA is defined as segment income (loss) from operations excluding (i) depreciation and amortization, (ii) share-based compensation, (iii) restructuring charges, and (iv) impairment. Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA are key performance measures that our management uses to assess our financial performance as well as for internal planning and forecasting purposes. We consider Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA to be meaningful performance measures for investors to evaluate our operating performance and to compare the financial results"

**3. Segment Definitions for Space, Connectivity, and AI**
The notes to the consolidated financial statements define the three operating segments as follows:
> "Space Exploration Technologies Corp. and its wholly owned subsidiaries, collectively referred to as the “Company” or “SpaceX,” operate three segments – 
> (i) the Space segment designs, manufactures, and launches reusable rockets to provide high cadence, reliable, and affordable access to space at unprecedented scale, 
> (ii) the Connectivity segment operates a worldwide high-speed, low-latency broadband network powered by thousands of Starlink satellites in Low-Earth Orbit, delivering connectivity to millions of consumer, enterprise, and government customers through our Starlink offering, and 
> (iii) the AI segment operates a vertically integrated AI platform spanning a frontier LLM Grok, AI solutions for consumer and enterprise customers, X — a real-time information,"

**4. Caveats About Comparing Segment Economics & Shared Costs / Common-Control Accounting**
Based on the provided text chunks, there is **no specific disclosure** discussing caveats about comparing segment economics against one another, nor is there any discussion of how shared costs or common-control accounting affects segment results. The text focuses primarily on the standalone definitions, reconciliations, and year-over-year drivers for each segment individually.

steps.reasoning_content:
I have enough information to answer the question. Let me also check if there's any additional context about the common-control accounting and how it affects segment results, since the question mentions "comparing segment economics."

Actually, I have enough information. Let me structure my answer:

1. Identify each add-back relative to segment operating income:
   - Depreciation and amortization
   - Share-based compensation
   - Restructuring charges
   - Impairment

2. Explain why understanding the construction matters before comparing segment economics:
   - The add-backs are not symmetric across segments (e.g., AI segment has high D&A from xAI's compute infrastructure, restructuring charges are concentrated in AI segment, etc.)
   - Stock-based compensation is a real economic cost (dilution) but is excluded
   - The metric excludes interest, taxes, and other corporate items that may be allocated differently
   - The recast consolidated entity includes xAI and X Holdings under common-control accounting, so historical segment figures don't reflect standalone operations
   - Different capital intensity across segments means D&A add-backs disproportionately benefit capital-intensive segments
   - Restructuring and impairment are episodic and may distort period-over-period comparisons
   - The metric is not comparable to peers who may calculate differently

Let me also note the specific numbers from the reconciliation tables to illustrate the asymmetry.

Looking at the Q1 2026 reconciliation:
- Space: Operating loss $(662)M → Segment Adjusted EBITDA $(351)M (improvement of $311M from add-backs)
- Connectivity: Operating income $1,188M → Segment Adjusted EBITDA $2,087M (improvement of $899M from add-backs)
- AI: Operating loss $(2,469)M → Segment Adjusted EBITDA $(609)M (improvement of $1,860M from add-backs)

The AI segment gets the largest benefit from add-backs, primarily from D&A ($1,493M) and SBC ($378M). This is because AI has the most capital-intensive infrastructure (xAI's compute) and the most aggressive equity compensation.

For FY2025:
- Space: Operating loss $(657)M → Segment Adjusted EBITDA $653M (improvement of $1,310M)
- Connectivity: Operating income $4,423M → Segment Adjusted EBITDA $7,168M (improvement of $2,745M)
- AI: Operating loss $(6,355)M → Segment Adjusted EBITDA $(1,237)M (improvement of $5,118M)

The AI segment's improvement is enormous - $5.1B of add-backs, primarily D&A ($3,568M) and SBC ($1,063M) and restructuring ($487M).

This shows that the add-backs disproportionately benefit the AI segment, which has the most capital-intensive operations and the most aggressive equity compensation. Without understanding this, an investor might conclude that the AI segment is "almost breakeven" when in fact it's burning billions in cash through capex (which becomes D&A) and dilution (through SBC).

Let me also note that the recast consolidated entity includes xAI and X Holdings under common-control accounting, so historical segment figures don't reflect standalone operations.

Now let me write up the final answer.

arguments.final_result:
## Segment Adjusted EBITDA: Construction and Why It Matters

### 1. Definition (as disclosed in the S-1)

The filing defines **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** as **segment income (loss) from operations** excluding four items:

1. **Depreciation and amortization**
2. **Share-based compensation**
3. **Restructuring charges**
4. **Impairment**

Management states these measures are used "to assess our financial performance as well as for internal planning and forecasting purposes" and are presented to help investors "evaluate our operating performance and to compare the financial results." The filing also explicitly cautions that other companies "may calculate Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA differently than we do or may not calculate them at all, limiting their usefulness as comparative measures," and that the measures "should not be considered in isolation or as a substitute for net income (loss), income (loss) from operations, or any other measure calculated in accordance with GAAP."

### 2. Each Add-Back Relative to Segment Operating Income

Starting from segment income (loss) from operations (the GAAP starting point), the four add-backs are:

| Add-back | What it captures | Economic nature |
|---|---|---|
| **Depreciation & amortization** | Non-cash allocation of historical capex (launch vehicles, Starlink satellites, xAI compute infrastructure, acquired intangibles) | Real cash outflow occurred earlier; D&A is a proxy for prior-period capital intensity, not a "free" cost |
| **Share-based compensation** | Equity granted to employees (heavily concentrated in the AI segment post-xAI merger) | Real economic cost via shareholder dilution; non-cash but recurring and dilutive |
| **Restructuring charges** | Workforce reductions, lease exits, asset write-offs tied to reorganization (concentrated in AI segment, e.g., $487M in FY2025) | Episodic but reflects real cash and economic costs of reshaping the business |
| **Impairment** | Write-downs of long-lived assets, goodwill, or investments (e.g., $3,775M in AI in FY2023; $38M in Space in FY2025) | Non-cash but signals prior over-investment or asset value deterioration |

Note that Segment Adjusted EBITDA is **narrower** than the company-wide Adjusted EBITDA definition. Company-wide Adjusted EBITDA also excludes interest expense, interest income, other income (expense), and provision for income taxes — but those items sit below the segment line and therefore do not appear in the segment-level reconciliation.

### 3. Why Understanding the Construction Matters Before Comparing Segment Economics

The four add-backs are **not symmetric across segments**, so naive comparison of Segment Adjusted EBITDA across Space, Connectivity, and AI can be highly misleading. Several issues stand out:

**(a) Capital intensity is captured very differently across segments.** D&A is by far the largest add-back, and it is concentrated in the AI segment because of xAI's compute infrastructure build-out. In FY2025, D&A added back $3,568M to AI versus $2,376M to Connectivity and $757M to Space. Excluding D&A makes the AI segment's economics look far better than its cash consumption, because the underlying capex that generated that D&A was real cash spent in prior (and current) periods. An investor comparing Segment Adjusted EBITDA across segments without recognizing this would systematically overstate AI's relative profitability versus Space and Connectivity.

**(b) Share-based compensation is heavily skewed to AI.** SBC added back $1,063M to AI in FY2025 versus $515M to Space and $369M to Connectivity. SBC is a real, dilutive economic cost. Excluding it flatters the AI segment disproportionately, especially because the recast consolidated entity includes xAI (merged February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025) under common-control accounting — meaning the AI segment's SBC reflects equity-heavy compensation cultures imported from those entities.

**(c) Restructuring and impairment are episodic and segment-specific.** Restructuring charges are essentially an AI-segment phenomenon ($487M in FY2025, $213M in FY2024, $237M in FY2023; zero in Space and Connectivity across all periods shown). Impairment is also lumpy — $3,775M in AI in FY2023 alone. Because these items are excluded, period-over-period Segment Adjusted EBITDA comparisons can mask large one-time economic events that genuinely affected segment value.

**(d) The recast consolidated entity distorts historical comparability.** The S-1 retrospectively recasts all historical periods to include xAI and X Holdings under common-control accounting. No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations. Segment Adjusted EBITDA for the AI segment in 2023–2024 therefore reflects xAI/X economics that did not exist within SpaceX at that time, and the Space and Connectivity segments' historical figures may also reflect allocations that changed as a result of the recast. Comparing segment economics across years without understanding this recast basis can produce false trend signals.

**(e) The metric excludes items that may be allocated inconsistently.** Segment Adjusted EBITDA starts from segment income (loss) from operations, which already reflects the company's internal cost-allocation methodology. Corporate-level items (interest, taxes, other income/expense) are excluded entirely. Two segments with identical Segment Adjusted EBITDA could have very different true economics once capital structure, tax position, and corporate overhead allocations are considered.

**(f) Peer comparability is limited by construction differences.** The filing itself warns that peers "may calculate Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA differently than we do or may not calculate them at all." A peer's "Adjusted EBITDA" may include or exclude different items (e.g., some peers add back transaction costs, litigation, or stock-based comp differently), so cross-company segment comparisons require line-by-line reconciliation rather than headline comparison.

### 4. Illustrative Magnitude of the Distortion (FY2025)

| Segment (FY2025, $M) | Operating income (loss) | Total add-backs | Segment Adjusted EBITDA | Add-backs as % of |op income| |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Space | (657) | 1,310 | 653 | n/m (turns a loss into a profit) |
| Connectivity | 4,423 | 2,745 | 7,168 | 62% |
| AI | (6,355) | 5,118 | (1,237) | 81% of the loss is "added back" |

The AI segment's $6.4B GAAP operating loss is reduced to a $1.2B Segment Adjusted EBITDA loss — an $5.1B improvement driven overwhelmingly by D&A ($3.6B) and SBC ($1.1B). Without understanding that those add-backs represent prior capex and ongoing dilution, an investor could materially misjudge the AI segment's cash and economic burn rate relative to Space and Connectivity.

### 5. Bottom Line

Segment Adjusted EBITDA is a useful supplemental lens, but its construction — particularly the exclusion of D&A and SBC, which are large and unevenly distributed across segments — means it should never be used to rank segment economics without also examining:
- segment-level capex and the cash conversion gap between D&A and actual capital spending,
- segment-level SBC and its dilutive impact,
- the episodic nature of restructuring and impairment by segment,
- the common-control recast that makes historical AI segment figures non-standalone, and
- the company's own cost-allocation methodology underlying segment operating income.

The filing's own cautionary language reinforces this: the measures "should not be considered in isolation or as a substitute for" GAAP operating income, and peer comparability is explicitly limited.

---

  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026 — Non-GAAP Measures (Adjusted EBITDA / Segment Adjusted EBITDA definitions, reconciliations, and limitations) and Segment Information notes"
    }
  ]
}

results.content:
## Segment Adjusted EBITDA: Construction and Why It Matters

### 1. Definition (as disclosed in the S-1)

The filing defines **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** as **segment income (loss) from operations** excluding four items:

1. **Depreciation and amortization**
2. **Share-based compensation**
3. **Restructuring charges**
4. **Impairment**

Management states these measures are used "to assess our financial performance as well as for internal planning and forecasting purposes" and are presented to help investors "evaluate our operating performance and to compare the financial results." The filing also explicitly cautions that other companies "may calculate Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA differently than we do or may not calculate them at all, limiting their usefulness as comparative measures," and that the measures "should not be considered in isolation or as a substitute for net income (loss), income (loss) from operations, or any other measure calculated in accordance with GAAP."

### 2. Each Add-Back Relative to Segment Operating Income

Starting from segment income (loss) from operations (the GAAP starting point), the four add-backs are:

| Add-back | What it captures | Economic nature |
|---|---|---|
| **Depreciation & amortization** | Non-cash allocation of historical capex (launch vehicles, Starlink satellites, xAI compute infrastructure, acquired intangibles) | Real cash outflow occurred earlier; D&A is a proxy for prior-period capital intensity, not a "free" cost |
| **Share-based compensation** | Equity granted to employees (heavily concentrated in the AI segment post-xAI merger) | Real economic cost via shareholder dilution; non-cash but recurring and dilutive |
| **Restructuring charges** | Workforce reductions, lease exits, asset write-offs tied to reorganization (concentrated in AI segment, e.g., $487M in FY2025) | Episodic but reflects real cash and economic costs of reshaping the business |
| **Impairment** | Write-downs of long-lived assets, goodwill, or investments (e.g., $3,775M in AI in FY2023; $38M in Space in FY2025) | Non-cash but signals prior over-investment or asset value deterioration |

Note that Segment Adjusted EBITDA is **narrower** than the company-wide Adjusted EBITDA definition. Company-wide Adjusted EBITDA also excludes interest expense, interest income, other income (expense), and provision for income taxes — but those items sit below the segment line and therefore do not appear in the segment-level reconciliation.

### 3. Why Understanding the Construction Matters Before Comparing Segment Economics

The four add-backs are **not symmetric across segments**, so naive comparison of Segment Adjusted EBITDA across Space, Connectivity, and AI can be highly misleading. Several issues stand out:

**(a) Capital intensity is captured very differently across segments.** D&A is by far the largest add-back, and it is concentrated in the AI segment because of xAI's compute infrastructure build-out. In FY2025, D&A added back $3,568M to AI versus $2,376M to Connectivity and $757M to Space. Excluding D&A makes the AI segment's economics look far better than its cash consumption, because the underlying capex that generated that D&A was real cash spent in prior (and current) periods. An investor comparing Segment Adjusted EBITDA across segments without recognizing this would systematically overstate AI's relative profitability versus Space and Connectivity.

**(b) Share-based compensation is heavily skewed to AI.** SBC added back $1,063M to AI in FY2025 versus $515M to Space and $369M to Connectivity. SBC is a real, dilutive economic cost. Excluding it flatters the AI segment disproportionately, especially because the recast consolidated entity includes xAI (merged February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025) under common-control accounting — meaning the AI segment's SBC reflects equity-heavy compensation cultures imported from those entities.

**(c) Restructuring and impairment are episodic and segment-specific.** Restructuring charges are essentially an AI-segment phenomenon ($487M in FY2025, $213M in FY2024, $237M in FY2023; zero in Space and Connectivity across all periods shown). Impairment is also lumpy — $3,775M in AI in FY2023 alone. Because these items are excluded, period-over-period Segment Adjusted EBITDA comparisons can mask large one-time economic events that genuinely affected segment value.

**(d) The recast consolidated entity distorts historical comparability.** The S-1 retrospectively recasts all historical periods to include xAI and X Holdings under common-control accounting. No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations. Segment Adjusted EBITDA for the AI segment in 2023–2024 therefore reflects xAI/X economics that did not exist within SpaceX at that time, and the Space and Connectivity segments' historical figures may also reflect allocations that changed as a result of the recast. Comparing segment economics across years without understanding this recast basis can produce false trend signals.

**(e) The metric excludes items that may be allocated inconsistently.** Segment Adjusted EBITDA starts from segment income (loss) from operations, which already reflects the company's internal cost-allocation methodology. Corporate-level items (interest, taxes, other income/expense) are excluded entirely. Two segments with identical Segment Adjusted EBITDA could have very different true economics once capital structure, tax position, and corporate overhead allocations are considered.

**(f) Peer comparability is limited by construction differences.** The filing itself warns that peers "may calculate Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA differently than we do or may not calculate them at all." A peer's "Adjusted EBITDA" may include or exclude different items (e.g., some peers add back transaction costs, litigation, or stock-based comp differently), so cross-company segment comparisons require line-by-line reconciliation rather than headline comparison.

### 4. Illustrative Magnitude of the Distortion (FY2025)

| Segment (FY2025, $M) | Operating income (loss) | Total add-backs | Segment Adjusted EBITDA | Add-backs as % of |op income| |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Space | (657) | 1,310 | 653 | n/m (turns a loss into a profit) |
| Connectivity | 4,423 | 2,745 | 7,168 | 62% |
| AI | (6,355) | 5,118 | (1,237) | 81% of the loss is "added back" |

The AI segment's $6.4B GAAP operating loss is reduced to a $1.2B Segment Adjusted EBITDA loss — an $5.1B improvement driven overwhelmingly by D&A ($3.6B) and SBC ($1.1B). Without understanding that those add-backs represent prior capex and ongoing dilution, an investor could materially misjudge the AI segment's cash and economic burn rate relative to Space and Connectivity.

### 5. Bottom Line

Segment Adjusted EBITDA is a useful supplemental lens, but its construction — particularly the exclusion of D&A and SBC, which are large and unevenly distributed across segments — means it should never be used to rank segment economics without also examining:
- segment-level capex and the cash conversion gap between D&A and actual capital spending,
- segment-level SBC and its dilutive impact,
- the episodic nature of restructuring and impairment by segment,
- the common-control recast that makes historical AI segment figures non-standalone, and
- the company's own cost-allocation methodology underlying segment operating income.

The filing's own cautionary language reinforces this: the measures "should not be considered in isolation or as a substitute for" GAAP operating income, and peer comparability is explicitly limited.
